questions.question: What does a Java record automatically generate?
options: Constructor, getters with 'get' prefix, equals, hashCode, toString
Constructor, accessor methods (no prefix), equals, hashCode, toString
Constructor, getters, setters, equals, hashCode, toString
Only a constructor and toString
questions.explanation: Records generate a canonical constructor, accessor methods named after the components (no 'get' prefix), and equals(), hashCode(), toString() based on all components.

questions.question: Which is true about a compact constructor in a record?
options: It must explicitly assign all fields
It cannot modify parameters
Field assignments happen automatically after the compact constructor body
It replaces the canonical constructor entirely
questions.explanation: In a compact constructor, you can validate and normalize parameters. The actual field assignments (this.x = x) happen implicitly after the compact constructor body executes.

questions.question: Can a record implement an interface?
options: No — records cannot have any additional methods
Yes — records can implement interfaces and add methods
Only marker interfaces (no methods)
Only sealed interfaces
questions.explanation: Records can implement interfaces, including ones with methods. They can also define additional instance methods, static methods, and static fields. They just cannot extend classes or add instance fields.

questions.question: What modifier must every permitted subclass of a sealed class have?
options: abstract
final, sealed, or non-sealed
public
static
questions.explanation: Every permitted subclass must be declared as final (no further subclassing), sealed (restricts its own subclasses), or non-sealed (opens the hierarchy). This ensures the sealed contract is explicit.

questions.question: Why are records ideal as implementations of sealed interfaces?
options: Records are mutable, which sealed interfaces require
Records are implicitly final, satisfying the sealed contract without extra modifiers
Records can extend other classes within the sealed hierarchy
Records have better performance than regular classes
questions.explanation: Records are implicitly final, so they naturally satisfy the requirement that permitted subtypes must be final, sealed, or non-sealed. Combined with auto-generated equals/hashCode, they're perfect for algebraic data types.
